heroText: Damascus is a capital city in Syria, connecting local landmarks, event context, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

highlights.name: Damascus
highlights.text: Damascus is a capital city in Syria, connecting local landmarks, event context, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

history.label: 14th century
history.text: A number of Akkadian spellings are found in the Amarna letters, from the 14th century BC: Dimašqa (𒁲𒈦𒋡, 𒁲𒈦𒀸𒋡), and Dimašqì (𒁲𒈦𒀸𒄀).

history.label: 15th century
history.text: Late Bronze Age The name of Damascus first appeared in the geographical list of Thutmose III as tjmsqw (𓍘𓄟𓈎𓅱𓈉 and 𓍘𓄟𓊃𓐰𓈎𓅱) in the 15th century BC.

history.label: Modern city
history.text: In Egyptian, the prefix Ta- (𓍘) often functions as a feminine article or a designation for "the land/place of." The element -ms (𓄟) is the main element of interpretation using the "three fox skins".

history.label: Regional role
history.text: For a scribe, identifying the city by the "Hills" provides a logical geographical marker.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The Egyptian /T/ became Semitic Amorite /D/. Iron Age It is attested as Ša-imerišu (𒃻𒆳𒀲𒋙) in Neo-Assyrian Akkadian.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Later Aramaic Damašq (𐡃𐡌𐡔𐡒‎) in Old Aramaic and Dammeśeq (דַּמֶּשֶׂק) in Biblical Hebrew, spellings of the name often include an intrusive resh (letter r), perhaps influenced by the root dr, meaning "dwelling".

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The Qumranic Darmeśeq (דרמשק), and Darmsûq (ܕܪܡܣܘܩ) in Syriac, meaning "a well-watered land".

history.label: Regional role
history.text: In Greek mythology, there are three explanations for the city's name Damaskós (Δαμασκός).

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Another says it was named after Damaskos, the son of Hermes and the nymph Alimede, who traveled from Arcadia to Syria and founded a city bearing his name.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: A third version holds that Damaskos was a man who, after Dionysus made Syria fertile with vineyards, cut them down with an axe.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Thus, the English and Latin name of the city is Damascus, which was imported from Greek Δαμασκός.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The city is also known as aš-Šām by the citizens of Damascus, of Syria and other Arab neighbors and Turkey (Şam).

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The variation ش ء م (š-ʾ-m'), of the more typical ش م ل (š-m-l), is also attested in Old South Arabian, 𐩦𐩱𐩣 (šʾm), with the same semantic development.
